speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sonia, you were talking about your father becoming ill and wasting away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he just didn't feel like doing anything. He just sat in the house. Finally, he got very sick. And we didn't have a doctor, as I said. Just this guy-- this-- came because he was, like, a neighbor of my-- of my grandparents. So he came, and he was elderly already. But you couldn't get any doctors, or any medication, or anything. And he finally-- they finally said that he had pneumonia. And then his stomach got so big, you know, and-- from uremia. His kidneys got infected or whatever. And it took about two weeks, and he passed away, which was on the-- on May 2, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, let's see. At that time, you-- they-- you couldn't have a funeral. You could only have-- you know, you had to just somehow bring the body to the cemetery. And they buried it there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father, my father-- my, my family was very prominent, my grandparents and my, from my father's side. So the Jewish committee saw to it that they brought the-- you know, there's like a-- what do they call it? They-- Mortuary?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There, there was no mortuary. They just put the body on the-- on the floor. And they washed it, and they put white things on it, and you know, they put it on a-- on a board. And like this, they took it to the cemetery. But-- and, you know, people usually had to take them whichever way you could. But because it was my father and his friend was in the committee, he saw to it that-- you know, they used to have like a-- it was like a wagon, and it was horse-drawn. And you know, like a black, you know, curtains, and you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were-- they brought this for my father to be taken to the cemetery. But we couldn't go through the streets. We had to go through back alleys, you know, from one back alley to another alley to the cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and at that time, I-- my-- you know, the diggers made a hole. And my grandfather went down into the grave. And he put my father down himself. And he said, I will never think that I'm going to live to bury my-- my son in law. [CRYING] My-- I'm old, but he's young. And, and, you know, we just went home. And it was-- everything went on, you know. At that time, my, my brother afterwards, he, he was about 13 years old. He suffered so much. And we had some Polish friends, that they said that they're going to-- they want my brother to work for them on the farm for the summer. And this was summer of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he worked there. And every, every, Friday he, he would come home for a, for Saturday. And they would also, they would give him all kinds of food to bring home. So he-- so he worked. And, you know, during the week, he was there. And Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, he was home. Monday he went back. And that helped also out the family for, you know, for food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and, you know, this was, we had to worry about my grandparents at that time. And then my grandfather was, at the time, you know, he was just devastated after my father died. And also, at-- by that time, we already found out that my, my uncle, one of my uncles that went to Posen was dead, that my uncle, my mother's brother, was dead. By that time, we found all these things out. And anyway, I worked in this shop. And in August of, of '42, all of a sudden in the morning-- and there were rumors around that they're going-- you know, that's going to happen. And all of the sudden, they're knock on all the doors and took all the Jews out. And they took them to, to the temple. And in the temple, in the yard of the temple.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the Jews were just standing, standing there. And then after maybe five, six hours, we were all standing there, and they came. Let's see. How do you say it? Like a-- that all the people that worked in the shop should go on the other side. So I-- I didn't want to go away from my family. And, you know, all the-- my uncle, the one that, that was still-- the one that-- the tailor, he was there. And, and all the other people that worked in that-- in that shop, which was a lot of people, maybe 500, 600 people worked in that shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they all were taken to the Lodz ghetto. And I didn't want to go away from my grand-- from my parents and my grandparents. And they dragged me because I worked in the shop that I had to go too. They had to take me to the ghetto. And all the time when I was in the ghetto, I was just under, you know, what they told us, that they're taking us to this ghetto and that-- you know, to this place, and that, and-- and all the other people are going to other places where they're also going to work, and you know, and survive the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, while I was in the ghetto-- when I came to the ghetto, it was really terrible because I, I was about-- let's see, how old was I?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. Maybe 17, 18 years old, and I was all by myself. And when I came to the ghetto, I-- during the-- during the war, right after the war, started I-- my father had relatives in Lodz. And we went there. You know, we walked, from our-- which was about maybe 40 kilometers. So my father and I walked to Lodz. And at that time, they gave us clothes and things like this to take back because our house was burned. We had nothing left. So they gave us some clothes and some things, you know, for, for my mother, for me, for my sister. And we walked home. So when I got to the ghetto, I knew where my relatives lived. So when I got to the ghetto, they, they held us in, like, in a prison for I don't know, maybe a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we slept on, you know, there was these wood beds, and it was just like a prison, worse than a prison. So we were there for awhile, and then they said that, you know, they gave us housing. And they took like-- I was, my uncle, I told you my uncle also came to the Lodz ghetto because he worked in that shop, and three of his sisters, younger sisters. And they were-- one was my age, and two were younger than my-- than, than I.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they gave us a place to live together with my uncle because I didn't, you know-- my uncle said, no. You have to be with us, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. I have to take care of you, because after the war, you know, if I won't take good care of you, my wife is, you know, like my aunt, you know? He says, she made me responsible for you. So you stay-- you live with me. So we lived together in one room, four people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had to-- one-- this was-- they gave us housing. And then I went to my aunt, one of my aunts, that gave us clothes at that time. She was still in the ghetto at that time. And she gave me bedding, and sheets, and pillowcases, and things like this. So I, I had that. And pots and pans, she gave me some things. So I had that. And not long after when we were there, they-- we had to come to a place where they give you-- to find work for you. So that was like a, it was called in German, [GERMAN]. And it was like an office where you-- they gave you jobs.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were standing in line from the-- you know, from our city for jobs. So 50-- it was one-- we was standing in line and then one person came. He was dressed in like, a-- like a-- the police in the in Lodz. I'm sure you heard this from, from your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had these special hats and things like this. And so they came. One of these, like a big chief came with the medals and everything. And he picked out 15 girls to work for him. And I was among the 15 girls. And he asked me my name, and I told him my name's Shava Konsons. So he says, Shava Konsons, I was engaged to your cousin. My cousin was no longer in the ghetto, they already sent her out. But he was engaged to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So since-- from that time on, he was really wonderful to me. He Saved my life I don't know how many times over. He gave me a job, and he had like a-- he lived in, in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was like a suburb of Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was-- and I got a job there in his factory. And they made straw, straw mattresses. So I worked at that time over there. And he-- you know, this was his-- he was the chief-- the chief over this factor. And I worked there, and he was really, very nice to all of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, this lasted for a while. And, and then this factory was closed. And then we worked, like, in a field where they already took out all the potatoes, you know? Just to pick-- put together the potato that were left over. And, you know, we did that, you know? And we had to put it in sacks and put it on one side. And when this-- this was finished, and again, I had nothing to do. So he had-- he had this office. He was like, the-- at that time, I didn't know that. But he was like, very high ranking in the Jewish police in the ghetto. So he took me to his work in his office. So I was like, in debt, was-- how do you say that in, in English? Where they cut trees? [POLISH] in Polish. I don't know how they say in English, like a lumberyard. So I worked in the-- and he took me. And this was like in the middle of the city, and he took me to work in this lumberyard. So I worked sometimes in the lumberyard outside, you know? Sometimes, most of the time, in the office.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, like, say, if you-- they didn't have computers and things like this. So we had to take, let's say from this office, put paper in this office, or you know, to give to this guy or, you know, like this. That's what I did. And, and I had it very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I wasn't happy. Yeah. And then, I wasn't happy where I lived because it was far away from where I worked. And it was cold there, and I-- and I didn't-- and you know, and many times, my uncle had to get upset with his sisters because of me, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the one that, you know, it was older than they, just the other sister. And I was the one that they had to-- you know, I had to go to work. Beside them, we had to cook and schlep water up the steps and things like this. And he would fight with his sisters that they leave-- you know, I would feel that I have to do it. You know, they're going to come home, you know, and my uncle's going to come home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so one time I was very unhappy and I was crying. And he came up to me and says, what's the matter with you? You know, why are you crying? So I told him, I'm unhappy where I live. So there was one woman that lived about four blocks away from the-- from the-- from the factory, from the job. And he talked to her. And he told her to give me a room in her house. And she did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was like from the-- from the-- it was like if they had to go into their house, that it was open like this, and this was the room that they gave me. In order for me to have privacy, he sent some people and made a door. And I lived there till we got-- we got sent out from the ghetto. But while there, I-- you know, in the ghetto, there was-- there was, like, a gentleman's agreement. If I worked in this factory, let's say, and they always got orders to take out, let's say, 2,000 people, 500 people, 5,000 people, they, they always would. And and they just came, and they took away the children, the sick people, the thing-- you know, and always eliminated people from the ghetto. And then it-- they took already whoever. So they made up, let's say, if somebody worked the, in another factory, so they put you on a list. You know, they always had to make these lists to send people out because constantly, they were sending people out. And since I was by myself in the ghetto-- so the people that were by themselves, they were first to go because they figured you're not breaking up a family, or you're not making unhappy a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he would come to me and would say, Shava, I have to put you on the list, but you're not going to go. But I have to put you because all these people-- because they had to put out a list on the-- on the wall that tomorrow those, and those, and those people had to go into this and this place to go-- to be deported. So he always put me on the list but he-- you know, but he always did. You know, he says, tomorrow morning, instead of coming here, you go to my house. And he lived in [NON-ENGLISH]. It's like in the suburb. So I would go there, and I would stay there, let's say, two days, three days. And I-- you know, he had a garden. They called it [NON-ENGLISH]. So I worked over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the time-- you know, two, three days went by, when it was already-- when all these people already sent away and, you know, things. So they took away the the-- when you were on the list, they took away your ration cards. And you had nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came back, you know, when it was quieted down, he told me that it's OK for me to come back to the-- to the shop to work. And on the way there, I was-- you know, we were going in a-- in a-- not-- it wasn't a bus. It was like a trolley.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was on the trolley to go to, to work, and they took everybody off the trolley and me too. And they put us on a place where there was a big, big, like a empty place. And they usually-- they called it the potato-- the potato plaza. Not plaza, you know, Platz. So they took me there. And from there, they took everybody out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I saw some people there. They were guarding us, this Jewish police. And I saw somebody that's always used to come into the office where I used to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him what happened, that I was in-- you know, this man's name was Adic Rosen-- that I was at Adic Rosen's house. And I told him the whole thing. And that-- and that he expects me to come to the office. He told me it's already safe to come out, and they caught me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went there. And he came and he, he saw that, you know, what's happening to me. So he talked to somebody. You know, there were like little doors, different places where you could go in and out. So he told-- he talked to somebody and then he, he came to me and he told them, Shava, you go over there to that door in about a half hour. There's going to be a guy over there. He's going to let you out, and I'm going take you to my sister. And his sister didn't live too far from there. So he took me out from there, and, and I waited a half hour. And I walked over there, and this guy just let me out. And there was somebody waiting, and they took me to his sister. So I was in his sister house-- sister's house for a while, till, again, it was quiet. And, and I started working again. This, this whole thing, you know, between coming out, it was about three weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a cousin in-- in the ghetto. And she thought for sure that they took me away, and that-- you know, that's it. When I came there, you know, after I-- it was already quiet, I came there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she just couldn't believe it that I'm still alive. You know, she said, where were you? I was-- you know, I asked about you. I went to where you lived, and, you know, nobody knew what happened to you. And I thought for sure that they deported you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I was there and I didn't have any ration cards. But you know, I, I-- you know, I wasn't starving because he always brought me food. You know, let's say, if he sent me for soup, they get little, little tickets for soup. So he always gave me extra for me, and always-- you know. And after a while, they gave you your cards back. So again, you got, you know, the-- let's say, a bread a week. So they gave you back the bread and things like this. And I was to go to my cousin. And she used to help me out, too, because she worked in a place where they had-- they sold meat. And she was like a big shot there. So she always had, you know, you know, plenty of food in the house. So she helped me out, too. And this, you know, went on, like, in all the ghetto. A few times, I wasn't on the list, and a few times, you know, he told me I have-- I have to put you on the list. But, you know, I never went. Anyway, the last time, just before-- you know, in the meantime, it was cold. I had hardly anything to dress. I-- if-- and my head was constantly worried about my, my-- the rest of my family. Because I figured to myself, I'm here. My, my aunt gave me-- gave me bedding, and I have clothes because my cousin, the one that was a tailor, so he always made me clothes. And, you know, I had necessities. The only thing was very cold because the place that I have-- there was no place where to heat. But I would stay, you know, at night, I would stay with this, this Jewish couple till I went to bed. And in bed was OK. But I was constantly worried. And I said to myself, here I am in bed, and it's-- you know, I have a cover, and I have a blanket. And God only knows if my-- you know, I knew my father was dead. But my mother, and my sister, and my brother, what-- and if they have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's going on with them? If they can work that hard. If they could do-- because we used to get up five o'clock in the morning and go to work. And come home in the dark. If they can do all these things, you know? And it was constantly on my mind. I just had no peace of mind because constantly was-- this in my thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, in the meantime, you know, let's say, you just lived. And There was organizations in the ghetto. You made friends in the ghetto. You, you-- you know, you, you had meetings. And there's all these things were going on, you know, in the ghetto if you really wanted to, to be involved. And so I, I didn't have such a bad life in the ghetto. And of course, 1944, when they took everybody out. So they took, like, portions, let's say, of this street, of this section. And they-- the Jewish police would tell you, because I was in this, you know-- and where I worked, they had radio, that the underground, you know. And they would-- we would know exactly where the Russians are. We would know exactly what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, let's say, how long it might take them, that the Russians are staying at the-- at the one place, you know, I think, before Warsaw. And you know, and a lot of people were already liberated at that time. But when are they going to come and liberate us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, all of sudden, this orders came that-- to liquidate the ghetto. And they took it, like, section by section. So the same thing. I was with Mr. Rosen's cousins and other family. And he told me, today they're going to do your street. The, the street was [NON-ENGLISH]. And they're going to take out-- everybody away from [NON-ENGLISH]. And you go to-- tonight, you go to my cousin's house, and you stay there with them. And I did. And they took everybody out from my street. Then, they took different sections, you know, because there were people left over in the ghetto. And so was Mr. Rosen. Anyway, when this was already quieted down, he told me. It's OK to go back. Now, take my cousins. Because they're going to take her street. And they took-- and we went to my, my house-- my place where I lived, where this was supposed to be already safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they got me, and all his family at that time. And when we came-- you know, we came, and they took us to the-- to the-- to go to the train station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came, but he couldn't do anything anymore. He couldn't take us out because I was not the only one. Also, at that time, the second time, they caught the girls that I told you, two, two sisters that used to live there. They were also not home when they took the people on street. But they took them together with us at the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figured out, you know, in order-- when we went to Auschwitz at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they took us to the train, so we were together with my, my, the one that I worked for, with his cousin and her, her two children, her two daughters, because her husband was already not alive anymore either.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everyday in the ghetto, you saw people laying dead. All the time, you know, with swollen bellies. The children were not there anymore. Young people were not there anymore. Older people, you could see them always laying in the street. And they always used to go around with the wagons and pick up all the deads. And when this-- they liquidated the ghetto. At that time, they took me to-- took about three days, that we wound up in Auschwitz. And at that time, I told you that I took the name because I didn't want to be separated from-- so I figured when they going to ask me what my name is and all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in order, because, I-- my name with a K, and they were the A, so in order for us to be together, so I said my name is Adler. Anyway, when we got out on the other side, they weren't there anymore anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the, the, the cousins from my chief which had two daughters were together with me. And then, they over there-- I don't have to explain to you how it was. Go here, go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, tell me what you saw. Tell me what you saw when you got off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got off the train, there were probably, maybe a quarter of the people who were in the train were dead because it took about three days. And, and it was such a chaos with the SS, with the dogs. And with the people that were helping them. It was such a chaos. You didn't know where you're going, didn't know what's going on. They just hit you with these-- out, out from the train. And then they-- you came to a certain place, and they segregated you. You go here. You go there. And then we still-- you know, we didn't know where we were. And then we went through this-- they told us, they took to a one big barrack. And they said we should put all our stuff down there and take off all our clothes and just put it in a pile. And we walked out from there naked. Then, we were in a-- in a row where they cut off our hair. And when we came out of the other side without hair, I didn't recognize anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us-- you know, and we started, you know, calling names, you know? Because we didn't know who we were anymore. And those two, the mother with the two daughters were next to me, and we tried to be together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those other two girls I couldn't find anymore. And we were sitting outside in Auschwitz for about half a night, or-- naked in a-- in like, a-- in a field. In the morning, they assigned us to a barracks. So there were people in there already. And, let's say, when there were, where there were three, they put one. And everyone had to be four people in each one of these. And it was like three stories high.
